serving.description: 1 serving as side dish or filling
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Potato Masala as a side dish or prata filling is approximately 100g, equivalent to a small bowl or a generous spoonful used to fill one roti prata.

nutrient_source.title: Potato Masala — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) for Potato Masala (cr_id G00010337). All per_100g values are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Potato Masala as a composite dish. GI estimated at 70 based on boiled/mashed potato analogues (GI range 70–85) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The spice and fat content (11.3g/100g fat) may modestly lower effective GI versus plain boiled potato, but the estimate conservatively uses the lower bound of the boiled potato range. Glycemic load calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = 70 × 13.49 / 100 = 9.4 per 100g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 219 mg/100g with insufficient potassium and fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 219 mg/100g places this dish in the borderline-high range for hypertension; potassium (219 mg/100g) provides only marginal offset and dietary fibre (1.5 g/100g) is below the 3 g threshold, so individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods such as roti prata.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI potato base (~GI 70) with low dietary fibre (1.5 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Potato Masala is based on high-GI potato (estimated GI ~70) with only 1.5 g/100g dietary fibre, resulting in rapid postprandial glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion to 50–75 g, pair with high-fibre accompaniments, and consider substituting cauliflower or sweet potato to reduce glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated total fat (11.3 g/100g) and unusually high cholesterol (74 mg/100g) for a vegetable dish
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fat content of 11.3 g/100g and cholesterol of 74 mg/100g are elevated for a potato-based vegetable dish, suggesting significant cooking oil use and possibly egg content in some preparations; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request preparation with minimal oil, confirm the absence of egg if cholesterol-restricted, and limit portion size to manage saturated fat and cholesterol intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetable (potato) — solanine content may aggravate inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Potato is a nightshade vegetable containing solanine, which may trigger inflammatory responses in RA-sensitive individuals; those with confirmed nightshade sensitivity should avoid or strictly limit this dish, noting that turmeric in the recipe provides anti-inflammatory curcumin but is insufficient to offset the nightshade concern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Potato Masala (also known as Aloo Masala or Masala Kentang) is a spiced potato dish widely consumed in Singapore, particularly as a filling for roti prata or as a side dish in Indian and Malay cuisine contexts. It is prepared by boiling potatoes until soft, then stir-frying them with onions, green chillies, turmeric, mustard seeds, curry leaves, and salt in cooking oil to produce a fragrant, mildly spiced, semi-dry curry. The texture is soft and slightly chunky, with a warm golden-yellow colour from turmeric. The flavour profile is savoury, mildly spicy, and aromatic. Per 100g, it provides 171 kcal, 3.89g protein, 11.32g fat, 13.49g carbohydrate, 1.5g dietary fibre, and 219mg sodium. The relatively high fat content (11.3g/100g) reflects generous oil use in preparation. Cholesterol at 74mg/100g is unusually elevated for a vegetable dish and may indicate egg content in some commercial preparations. Clinically, this dish is a concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its high-GI potato base and low fibre content, for hypertension due to borderline sodium with insufficient potassium and fibre offset, and for cardiovascular disease due to elevated fat and cholesterol. It is also a nightshade-containing dish relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management. It is vegetarian, vegan, halal, and gluten-free in standard preparation.
common_user_queries: Is potato masala good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) for the exact dish Potato Masala (cr_id G00010337) with HPB-assigned confidence of 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using boiled/mashed potato as the closest approved analogue, which reduces GI confidence to medium for that specific field, but overall record confidence remains high given the exact HPB nutrient match.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Potato Masala is not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) as of 2026-04-16. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data beyond the reported fields are not available in the HPB Foodo record for this dish as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~70) based on boiled/mashed potato data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Potato Masala as a composite dish was found in approved sources. 4. Cholesterol at 74mg/100g is unusually high for a potato-based vegetable dish; this may reflect egg content in some commercial preparations of this dish. 5. The input record classifies this dish under food_group 'Grains and staples' and food_subgroup 'Baking ingredients', which appears to be a misclassification; this output reclassifies it as food_group 'Vegetables' / food_subgroup 'Cooked vegetable dishes' as more appropriate for a spiced potato preparation. 6. Input cultural.primary_cuisine was listed as 'Malay'; this output corrects to 'Indian / Singaporean' as Potato Masala (Aloo Masala) is of Indian culinary origin, widely adopted in Singapore across Indian and Malay food contexts.
